s2.q: Intrauterine Fetal Death (IUFD) is defined as fetal death in-utero after:
options: 16 weeks of pregnancy or fetus weight > 350 gm
20 weeks of pregnancy or fetus weight > 500 gm
24 weeks of pregnancy or fetus weight > 600 gm
28 weeks of pregnancy or fetus weight > 1000 gm
s2.explain: IUFD is death of the fetus in-utero after 20 weeks of pregnancy or if the fetus weight is > 500 gm.

s2.q: The weight threshold defining IUFD is:
s2.explain: IUFD is defined when the fetus weight is > 500 gm (in addition to gestational age > 20 weeks).

s2.q: Fetal death occurring before 20 weeks is classified as:
s2.explain: Fetal death before 20 weeks is classified as miscarriage or spontaneous abortion. IUFD specifically refers to death after 20 weeks or > 500 gm.

s2.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s2.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

s2.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings
s2.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.

s3.q: Which of the following is a maternal cause of IUFD?
s3.explain: Maternal causes include diabetes, SLE, antiphospholipid syndrome, infection, hypertension, preeclampsia, eclampsia, uterine rupture, maternal trauma/death, and thrombophilias.

s3.q: Fetal causes of IUFD include all EXCEPT:
s3.explain: Fetal causes include IUGR, congenital abnormality, genetic abnormality, infection (Parvovirus B19, CMV, Listeria), and hydrops. Cord accident is a placental cause.

s3.q: Which infection is associated with IUFD?
s3.explain: Fetal infections associated with IUFD include Parvovirus B19, CMV, and Listeria.

options: Cord accident, abruption, vasa previa, and placental insufficiency
s3.explain: Placental causes include cord accident, abruption, PROM, vasa previa, feto-maternal hemorrhage, and placental insufficiency.

s3.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s3.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

s4.q: Which of the following is a symptom of IUFD?
s4.explain: Symptoms of IUFD include history of the cause, gradual disappearance of pregnancy symptoms, breast milk secretion, failure of abdominal enlargement, and cessation of fetal movements.

s4.q: A key sign of IUFD on examination is:
options: Uterus larger than expected for dates
Uterus smaller than the period of amenorrhea
s4.explain: Signs of IUFD include uterus smaller than the period of amenorrhea, difficult palpation of fetal parts (due to absent muscle tone), and inaudible FHS by Doppler.

s4.q: In IUFD, fetal parts are difficult to palpate because of:
s4.explain: Difficult palpation of fetal parts is due to absent muscle tone after fetal death.

s4.q: Which clinical finding is most suggestive of this diagnosis?
options: Varies by condition — specific findings guide diagnosis
s4.explain: Clinical presentation differs by condition and guides diagnostic testing.

s4.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s4.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

s5.q: Spalding sign on X-ray in IUFD refers to:
options: Overlapping of fetal skull bones due to shrinkage of cerebrum
s5.explain: Spalding sign is overlapping of fetal skull bones due to shrinkage of cerebrum after fetal death.

s5.q: Robert sign on X-ray in IUFD refers to:
options: Presence of gas in fetal large vessels
s5.explain: Robert sign is the presence of gas in fetal large vessels.

s5.q: Ball sign on X-ray in IUFD refers to:
s5.explain: Ball sign is hyperflexion of fetal spine. The mnemonic SRB helps recall: Spalding (skull overlap), Robert (gas), Ball (spine).
Choice of investigation depends on the suspected condition.

s5.q: Which of the following is a key concept?
options: It is essential for understanding the condition
It is rarely relevant in clinical practice
It applies only to specific populations
It has been replaced by newer classifications
s5.explain: Understanding key concepts is fundamental to clinical management.

s6.q: After how many weeks of fetal death does the risk of maternal DIC and hypofibrinogenemia rise sharply?
s6.explain: Once fetal death has persisted for > 3 weeks, the risk of maternal DIC and hypofibrinogenemia rises sharply.

s6.q: Complications of IUFD include all EXCEPT:
s6.explain: Complications of IUFD include intrauterine infection and DIC/hypofibrinogenemia (after 3 weeks). Pulmonary hypoplasia is a complication of PROM.

s6.q: Why is DIC a concern in prolonged IUFD?
options: The dead fetus releases thromboplastin into maternal circulation
The placenta stops producing clotting factors
Fetal bone marrow releases thrombogenic substances
s6.explain: DIC occurs because the retained dead fetus releases thromboplastin into the maternal circulation, triggering disseminated intravascular coagulation. This is why delivery is usually offered.

s6.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings
s6.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.

s6.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s6.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

s7.q: The first principle in management of IUFD is:
options: Termination of pregnancy should be offered after diagnosis
s7.explain: The first principle is that termination of pregnancy should be offered after diagnosis.

s7.q: Induction of labor for IUFD is accomplished with:
options: Preinduction cervical ripening followed by IV oxytocin
s7.explain: Induction of labor can be accomplished with preinduction cervical ripening followed by intravenous oxytocin.

s7.q: If induction of labor fails in IUFD management, what is the next step?
s7.explain: If induction fails, cesarean section is done.

options: Depends on the specific condition and severity
s7.explain: Management approach varies — some conditions require surgery, others medical or conservative care.

s7.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings
s7.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.
